1a7215b5bd18732c726ab98aa43e77305c30022e.reason: The HTTP handler fetch (registered at /fetch) reads the attacker-controlled query parameter 'url' via r.URL.Query().Get("url") into 'target' (main.go:12), then passes it unmodified to http.Get(target) (main.go:13). There is no validation, allowlist, scheme/host check, or any other sanitization between the source and the sink, and the handler is reachable directly via the registered route in main() (main.go:23). This is a classic unauthenticated SSRF: an attacker can make the server issue arbitrary outbound HTTP requests (e.g., to internal/metadata endpoints) and the response body is streamed back to the client via io.Copy(w, resp.Body) (main.go:19), enabling data exfiltration.

f68f31f7a921dd1b812e13c52e8c6df0fdd1e6b7.reason: The server is started with http.ListenAndServe(":8080", nil) at demo/vulnerable-app/ssrf/main.go:24, with no TLS configuration anywhere in the file (no tls.Config, no ListenAndServeTLS, no reverse proxy/TLS termination visible in this package). All traffic to the /fetch handler, including the SSRF target URL parameter, is served in plaintext over port 8080, matching the rule's definition of cleartext transmission (CWE-319). There is no sanitization or wrapping that adds TLS, so the flagged sink is reachable exactly as written.
